Results show that current Audio LLMs perform better on perception than on complex temporal and causal reasoning [175]. Trend: Audio Learns to Reason Audio reasoning is emerging from audio perception through the adaptation of LLM paradigms to acoustic signals. Recent progress shows that prompting, instruction tuning, and reasoning-oriented training can elicit multi-step inference, but current models still struggle with complex temporal and causal relationships. Future work will likely focus on improving reasoning faithfulness and consistency, strengthening temporal-causal modeling, and developing better training signals and benchmarks. 5.3.2 Visual Reasoning Definition. Visual reasoning refers to models ability to perform multi-step, compositional, and often abstract inference grounded in visual inputs [148, 176, 251, 677]. Unlike recognition-centric tasks, visual reasoning emphasizes how conclusions are derived from visual evidence, involving capabilities such as relational understanding [238, 243, 623], counting [242], causal inference [323, 420, 623], and commonsense reasoning [582, 699]. Methods. Early visual reasoning models were largely task-specific and symbolic or neuro-symbolic [676]. Benchmarks such as CLEVR [251] and GQA [238] inspired methods that modeled reasoning via functional programs [503, 511], scene graphs [214, 309, 488], and modular networks [17, 88]. Although effective in controlled settings, their reliance on synthetic data and predefined reasoning primitives limited real-world generalization. Vision-language foundation models shifted this paradigm by leveraging LLM reasoning. Systems such as Flamingo and BLIP-2 showed that pairing visual encoders with frozen LLMs can yield emergent visual reasoning, while instruction-tuned MLLMs (including the LLaVA series [14, 296, 345, 346], Qwen-VL series [29, 31], and InternVL series [97, 570]) demonstrate strong reasoning via prompting and chain-of-thought 29 supervision [206, 444, 508]. Architectures such as QvQ [521] further highlight how model design and reasoningoriented training shape visual reasoning. To improve robustness beyond supervised reasoning signals, recent work explores reinforcement learning (RL). Methods such as Reason-RFT [512], Point-RFT [418], Visionary-R1 [621], and VTool-R1 [612] apply RL-based fine-tuning to strengthen grounding, reasoning consistency, and tool-assisted multimodal reasoning, achieving improved performance on complex tasks including ChartXiv [594]. Visionary-R1 [621] trains VLMs with RL to encourage models to first interpret images and then reason, mitigating shortcut paths and improving performance against strong multimodal baselines such as GPT-4o [239] and Claude3.5-Sonnet [18]. Video reasoning introduces additional challenges due to temporal structure and event dependencies. Recent RL-based methods encourage temporally grounded reasoning, including Video-R1 [151] with T-GRPO [191], VideoRFT [564], VideoChat-R1/R1.5 [319, 650], and Time-R1 [584]. These approaches optimize temporalaware rewards to align reasoning with relevant video moments and improve long-video spatio-temporal inference. Open Problem: Reasoning Must Stay Grounded These trends suggest shift from step-wise chain-of-thought to more grounded multimodal reasoning with reinforcement-based training. Yet models still suffer from hallucinations, spurious correlations, and unfaithful reasoning. Recent work also frames reasoning as inference over latent chains, emphasizing diversity and uncertainty. Future progress will require tighter perception-reasoning integration, better grounding rewards, and benchmarks for both correctness and faithfulness. Benchmarks. Several benchmarks evaluate visual reasoning across perception and inference. MMMU [696] and MathVista [371] target reasoning over diverse visual formats and mathematical visual contexts, while MATH-V [559], Visual CoT [480], VisuLogic [645], MuSLR [641] and MMReason [666] focus on multimodal mathematical reasoning, grounded chain-of-thought reasoning, vision-centric reasoning, and open-ended multi-step reasoning. 5.3.3 Audio-Visual Reasoning Definition. Audio-visual reasoning requires models to infer why, when, and how events unfold by jointly interpreting video and audio. Beyond recognition or simple question answering, it emphasizes temporal alignment, causal inference, cross-modal grounding, and faithful rationale generation. Methods. Inspired by OpenAI-o1 [421] and DeepSeek-R1 [191], recent work starts to optimize reasoning explicitly for audio-video inputs. video-SALMONN-o1 [505] introduces process-level preference optimization for general audio-visual reasoning, while EchoInk-R1 [635], Omni-R1 [746], and AVATAR [284] apply RL-style post-training to improve grounded reasoning, self-correction, and long-horizon credit assignment. In parallel, ThinkOmni [190] transfers textual slow-thinking ability to omni-modal inputs without extra training. Another line targets reasoning reliability at inference time. AVCD [255] suppresses trimodal hallucination through contrastive decoding, while Fork-Merge Decoding [254] explicitly separates early audio-only and video-only reasoning before late fusion, reducing visually dominated shortcuts. At the current frontier, industrial systems are also becoming explicitly reasoning-centric. As OpenAI-o3 [423] extends strong reasoning to visual analysis, and Gemini 2.5/3.0 Pro [111] is positioned as thinking model with native multimodal understanding over audio and video. Qwen3-Omni-Thinking [639] also explicitly strengthens audio-visual reasoning in an open omni-modal setting. Together, these models indicate broader transition from multimodal perception to reasoning-centric omni systems. Benchmarks. Recent benchmarks increasingly target audiovisual reasoning rather than general multimodal understanding. OmniBench [325] and AVQA-R1-6K [635] capture audio-image reasoning, Daily-Omni [760] emphasizes temporal alignment, AURA [157] measures reasoning fidelity, JointAVBench [62] emphasizes Table 9 Comparison of representative audio-visual reasoning benchmarks. Recent benchmarks move beyond short closed-set QA, and increasingly emphasize temporal alignment, modality complementarity, hallucination diagnosis, and long-form reasoning. Benchmark # Source # QA Format Duration Domain OmniBench [325] AVQA-R1-6K [635] Daily-Omni [760] JointAVBench [62] OmniVideoBench [298] AV-ConfuseBench [671] 1.1K 1.9K 684 1.0K 628 59 1.1K 1.9K 1.2K 2.9K 1.0K 173 MCQA MCQA MCQA MCQA QA+rationale Mixed 9s 9s 30s / 60s 97s up to 30 min General Synthetic Daily Movie Long-form Confusion Table 10 Performance comparison of omni-modal models on Daily-Omni [760]. We report AV Align, Reasoning, and overall average accuracy (Avg, %). Method Size AV Align Reasoning Unified-IO-2 XL [370] VideoLLaMA2 [103] Ola [368] Qwen2.5-Omni-3B-Instruct [638] Qwen2.5-Omni-7B-Instruct [638] Qwen3-Omni-30B-A3B-Instruct [639] Qwen3-Omni-30B-A3B-Thinking [639] Gemini 2.5 Flash [111] 3B 7B 7B 3B 7B 30B 30B 30.25 35.71 40.34 50.84 48.32 66.81 65.97 73.82 21.71 34.29 69.71 70.29 73.14 81.14 80.57 81. Avg 28.32 35.17 50.71 60.23 62.07 71.85 73.60 73.06 cross-modal dependency, OmniVideoBench [298] extends evaluation to long-form audio-video understanding, and AV-ConfuseBench [671] tests resistance to visually plausible but audio-absent distractors. Trend: Towards Grounded and Faithful Audio-Visual Reasoning. Audio-visual reasoning is beginning to move beyond perception-oriented fusion toward MLLM-based reasoning over temporal, causal, and grounded evidence. Recent methods and benchmarks have expanded the scope from short synchronized QA to grounding, hallucination control, and long-video reasoning. However, the field is still at an early stage, as current systems remain limited in faithful cross-modal reasoning, robust temporal understanding, and reliable evaluation.

sections.title: 6 Audio-Visual Generation
sections.content: Audio-visual generation studies how to synthesize temporally aligned sound and imagery from text, images, video, or audio. We organize this section around three increasingly coupled regimes: conditional unimodal generation, cross-modal translation, and joint audio-visual generation, as summarized in Figure 9. The field has moved from composing strong single-modality generators to explicitly coupled and, more recently, unified architectures, with diffusion, flow matching, and multimodal transformers as the dominant design choices.

sections.title: 6.1 Conditional Audio/Visual Generation
sections.content: This part concerns generating or transforming one modality at time under external control (text, reference media, or instructions), without yet requiring full joint audio-video model. We treat conditional audio and conditional visual generation in separate subsections, both as building blocks for the cross-modal and joint settings that follow. 31 Figure 9 Organization of audio-visual generation tasks. We organize this section into conditional generation, cross-modal generation, and joint audio-visual generation, reflecting the transition from independent modality synthesis to tightly synchronized audio-video creation. 6.1.1 Conditional Audio Generation Definition. Conditional audio generation aims to synthesize or transform audio signals under external conditioning information, such as text descriptions, reference audio, mixture recordings, or editing instructions. Representative Tasks and Methods. We keep this single-modality subsection concise and focus on three common conditioning regimes: Text-conditioned audio, music, and speech generation synthesizes sound effects, ambient scenes, music, or speech from natural-language prompts. Representative approaches include latent diffusion models such as AudioLDM and Stable Audio Open [143, 344], codec-token language models such as AudioLM [46], and broader conditional frameworks such as AudioX [529]. Across these systems, neural audio tokenizers and latent representations remain central to fidelity, controllability, and efficiency. Conditional audio transformation and separation modify or decompose an existing recording, covering speech enhancement, source separation, remixing, and restoration. TF-GridNet [592] and Dual-Path Mamba [249] improve temporal modeling for separation, while Audio Prompt Tuning [365] moves toward promptable and open-set separation. In music, Band-Split RNN and Hybrid Transformers [376, 467] show how frequencystructured modeling supports instrument-level control. Instruction-guided audio editing uses natural language or reference cues to change selected attributes while preserving non-edited content. Representative systems include InstructAudioEdit [588], SAO-Instruct [537], Guiding Audio Editing with Audio Language Model [288], SteerMusic [419], Step-Audio-EditX [649], and EmoCorrector [357]. Related work on voice conversion, audio inpainting, and style transfer [313, 412, 440] points toward controllable audio editors that behave more like instruction-following generation systems. 6.1.2 Conditional Visual Generation Definition. Conditional visual generation synthesizes or transforms images and videos under external conditions, such as text prompts, reference images, structured controls, or editing instructions. Representative Tasks and Methods. Depending on the conditioning signal, this paradigm includes several related subtasks. 32 Text to Image/Video Generation. Text-to-image generation progressed from DALL-E [454] and Stable Diffusion [464] to stronger native multimodal and industrial systems, such as GPT-4o image generation [239], Seedream 5.0 Lite [52], HunyuanImage-3.0 [54], Qwen-Image [607], and JoyAI-Image [252]. Text-to-video generation likewise advanced from Make-A-Video [495] and Imagen Video [216] to newer foundation and product models, including Sora [422], Veo [120], Step-Video-T2V [380], HunyuanVideo-1.5 [278], Wan-2.1/2.2 [548], LongCat-Video [400], Any2caption [614], Hailuo 2.3 [408], Kling 3.0 [283], and Seedance 2.0 [51]. Recent work also extends short-clip synthesis toward long and online generation, including StreamingT2V [211], Stable Video Infinity [317], StreamDiT [274], Self-Forcing [234], and LoL [116]. Controllable Video Generation. Controllable generation increasingly disentangles appearance, structure, motion, and temporal continuation. ControlNet [718] and ControlVideo [725] showed that pretrained diffusion models can absorb structured controls without losing their generative prior. For image-to-video generation, Motion-I2V [489], HunyuanVideo-I2V [278], and Step-Video-TI2V [227] improve first-frame fidelity and motion control, while product systems such as Kling 3.0 [283], Veo [120], and Hailuo 2.3 [408] further strengthen practical shot-level control. For video interpolation, Generative Inbetweening [572], large-motion interpolation via I2V diffusion adaptation [250], and ViBiDSampler [659] adapt generative backbones for keyframe-conditioned temporal completion. Image/Video Editing. Image and video editing modify semantic content while preserving scene identity and temporal coherence. InstructPix2Pix [48] established instruction-based image editing, and Video-P2P [358] extended this idea to video. Recent systems, including Qwen-Image-Edit [607], JoyAI-Image-Edit [252], DreamVE [620], InsViE-1M [619], Nano-Banana [185], and Wan-2.7 [548] further combine understanding and editing in shared training pipelines.

sections.title: 6.2 Audio-Visual Cross-Modal Generation
sections.content: Cross-modal generation uses one realized sensory stream to drive another: adding sound to video, synthesizing video or motion from audio, animating still image with audio, or lifting audio into explicit 3D structure. The subsections below follow that scope from video-to-audio through audio-to-video, audio-synchronized image animation, and audio-driven 3D visual generation. 6.2.1 Video-to-Audio Generation Definition. Video-to-audio (V2A) generation adds plausible soundtrack to silent video, covering Foley, ambience, speech, or music depending on the scene [374]. The task is difficult because models must jointly satisfy semantic relevance, event-level synchronization, and acoustic plausibility under physical constraints. Compared with early Foley synthesis, current V2A models are increasingly expected to handle open-domain events, user control, and longer clips rather than only short impact sounds. Methods. Recent V2A methods are best grouped by how they enforce alignment and how much explicit reasoning they place before waveform generation. Alignment-first diffusion and flow models. Diff-Foley [374] established the modern recipe of contrastive AV pretraining plus latent diffusion conditioned on video features. FoleyCrafter [729] adds explicit onset conditioning for cleaner event timing, while FRIEREN and Tri-Ergon [295, 587] replace diffusion-style denoising with rectified or flow-matching objectives to improve efficiency without giving up fine-grained correspondence. Jointly trained and controllable V2A systems. The recent wave shifts the problem from narrow Foley generation to more controllable multimodal synthesis. MMAudio [101] shows the value of large-scale multimodal joint training, Smooth-Foley [726] adds text-guided style control, Kling-Foley [554] pushes quality with stronger multimodal diffusion transformer, and HunyuanVideo-Foley [479] scales the recipe to stronger video backbones and longer temporal windows. Reasoning-guided, open-world, and long-form generation. Another recent trend is to insert higher-level reasoning before audio decoding. ThinkSound [348] uses multimodal chain-of-thought to infer what should sound when, PrismAudio [347] uses multimodal LLMs to parse richer scene semantics, and DreamFoley [155] pushes toward higher-fidelity generation by leveraging scalable vision-language models. The frontier is now 33 Table 11 Representative benchmarks for Video-to-Audio generation. Benchmark Signal Focus Metrics VGGSound [70] Greatest Hits [427] AVSync15 [715] FoleyBench [131] AudioCanvas [347] VGGSound-Omni [118] Open-domain clips Contact clips Sync stress test Foley clips Open-world scenes V/T/A conditions Training/testing Impact/onset timing Temporal alignment Foley fidelity Multi-event soundscapes Omni-condition V2A FD, KL, IS, CLAP Onset, sync AVSync, VGGSync Human pref., sync, quality Semantic/spatial consistency Quality, align., instruction Table 12 Performance comparison of representative Video-to-Audio generation methods on the VGGSound-Test leaderboard. Metrics follow the official HunyuanVideo-Foley [479] evaluation protocol with PANNs-based FD/KL and Inception Score (IS). Best result in bold. Method Family FDP KLP Diff-Foley [374] FoleyCrafter [729] FRIEREN [587] MMAudio [101] ThinkSound [348] HunyuanVideo-Foley [479] Latent Diffusion Diffusion + Onset Control Rectified Flow Joint Multimodal Training MLLM-guided V2A Scaled Controllable V2A 6.34 4.12 4.18 2.89 2.70 2.51 6.15 4.01 4.16 2.82 2.38 2.17 IS 8.52 14.59 12.80 15.70 16. 16.87 moving beyond short clip synthesis: Omni2Sound [118] unifies video-text-to-audio generation under single any-to-audio framework, while ALIVE and Echoes Over Time [193, 494] target joint AV generation and length generalization over much longer horizons. AV-Link [202] shows that temporally aligned diffusion features can support unified V2A/A2V generation. Trend: Sound Needs Planning The main difference between recent V2A systems is no longer only diffusion versus flow matching. The more important split is whether the model directly maps video features to sound, or first constructs higher-level hypothesis about events, sources, and timing. This is why multimodal reasoning modules now matter: open-domain V2A increasingly looks like structured audiovisual planning followed by audio rendering, not just conditional denoising. Benchmarks. V2A benchmarks remain fragmented by sound type and synchronization granularity. VGGSound [70] and AudioSet [168] remain the default open-domain resources; Greatest Hits [427] stresses impact timing; AVSync15 [715] explicitly targets fine-grained synchronization; and newer resources such as FoleyBench, AudioCanvas, and VGGSound-Omni [118, 131, 347] move evaluation toward harder, more diverse, and longer-horizon settings. Common metrics now mix perceptual audio quality, semantic alignment, and synchronization quality rather than relying on single score. 6.2.2 Audio-to-Video Generation Definition. Audio-to-video (A2V) generation synthesizes video conditioned on sound, speech, or music. Compared with V2A, the ambiguity is larger: the same audio can support many visually plausible motions, so models must balance semantic faithfulness, rhythmic alignment, and controllability. Representative Tasks and Methods. A2V methods fall into three broad families. Backbone adaptation. These methods retrofit pretrained video generators with audio-conditioning modules. MusicInfuser [217] injects music features through cross-attention and low-rank adaptation, while Diverse and 34 Table 13 Representative benchmarks for Audio-to-Video generation. Dataset Scale Input Audio Visual Target Primary Use AIST++ [312] AIOZ-GDance [289] VoxCeleb2 [109] HDTF [732] VGGSound [70] 1.4K clips 19.8K clips 1M+ utterances 362 videos 200K clips Music Music Speech Speech Human motion/video Music-to-dance generation Group dance Talking face Talking face Choreography and formation control Identity-generalized speech animation High-resolution talking-head quality General sound Open-domain video Broad audio-conditioned video Aligned A2V [668] maps audio into the token space of pretrained text-to-video models. AV-Link [202] further shows that single temporally aligned framework can support both A2V and V2A generation. Motion-mediated pipelines. Rather than generating pixels directly, these systems first predict intermediate motion such as keypoints or SMPL trajectories and only then render video. ChoreoMuse [578] uses this decomposition for music-to-dance generation, and earlier works [764] follow similar two-stage design. The advantage is stronger beat alignment; the cost is higher pipeline complexity and weaker end-to-end flexibility. Identity-conditioned generation. Reference-image-based models reduce ambiguity by anchoring appearance. DiffTalk [483] produces speech-driven talking heads with landmark-guided diffusion, and AudCast [188] extends the setting to full-body avatars with cascaded diffusion transformers for facial motion, gestures, and body dynamics. Open Problem: Audio Underspecifies Video The central difficulty in A2V is not just quality but underdetermination: many videos can be faithful to the same soundtrack. Recent systems reduce this ambiguity with reference images, motion priors, or explicit control signals, but none fully resolves the trade-off between diversity and user steerability. In practice, controllability is becoming as important as realism because downstream use cases rarely want just any plausible video. Benchmarks. Benchmarks are task-specific. AIST++ [312] and AIOZ-GDance [289] dominate music-to-dance evaluation, VoxCeleb2 [109] and HDTF [732] are standard for speech-driven avatars, and VGGSound [70] remains the broadest open-domain benchmark for general A2V generation. Many recent systems additionally report zero-shot or curated in-the-wild evaluations [188]. 6.2.3 Audio-Synchronized Image Animation Definition. Audio-synchronized image animation, or audio-driven image-to-video (AI2V) generation, synthesizes video from reference image and driving audio while preserving identity and temporal coherence. Beyond the classical talking-head setting [115, 442], recent models also support general audio+image+text generation [165, 715], where text serves as auxiliary control over motion, emotion, or scene behavior. Representative Tasks and Methods. Recent audio-synchronized image animation methods can be divided into the following aspects: Human-centric AI2V. Most existing work remains human-centric. In talking-head and portrait animation, Wav2Lip [442] emphasizes lip accuracy; DiffTalk [483] and SadTalker [723] add head pose and expression; EMO [525], VASA-1 [643], Hallo2 [114], Hallo3 [115], FantasyTalking [562], and StableAvatar [534] further improve expressiveness, duration, and fidelity. Beyond talking heads, EchoMimicV2/V3 [403, 404] extends to semi-body animation, EMO2 [526] adds gesture-aware control, and OmniAvatar [159], OmniHuman1 [330], HuMo [80], AudCast [188], and Playmate2 [382] expand toward full-body, multi-character, and richer human motion. Recent industrial systems such as HunyuanVideo-Avatar [93], Kling-Avatar [73, 130], and JoyAvatar [567] further combine audio, image, and optional text to improve controllability and long-form avatar generation. 35 Table 14 Representative datasets and evaluation benchmarks for audio-driven image animation. Dataset / Benchmark Scale Target Primary Use VoxCeleb2 [109] LRS2 [498] LRS3 [3] HDTF [732] CelebV-HQ [765] TalkVid-Bench [85] TalkVerse [590] TalkCuts [72] MIT [769] THEval [447] EvalTalker [756] 1.13M utter., 2.44k 144k utter. 400h+ 15.8h, 300+ subj. 35.7k clips 1,244h + 500 eval clips 2.3M clips, 6.3k 164k clips, 500h+ 12h, 24 speakers 85k generated videos 5,492 samples Face Face Face Portrait Portrait Talking head Single-person Multi-human Multi-human Talking head Multi-human Identity generalization Lip-sync alignment AV speech alignment Talking-head synthesis Expressive portrait animation Fairness-aware evaluation Minute-long generation Multi-shot speech videos Multi-speaker interaction Fine-grained evaluation Perceptual quality assessment General AI2V. General AI2V is still much smaller than the human-centric branch, but it is not empty. ASVA [715] explicitly formulates static-image animation from audio across multiple object and scene classes, and KeyVID [574] improves this setting with keyframe-aware generation for highly dynamic motions. Scaling Up ASVA [716] extends the task toward open-domain audio-synchronized visual animation, while TIA2V [737] and Syncphony [499] achieve more controllable general video generation. In industry, Wan-S2V [165] sits near the boundary between human-centric and general AI2V, while broader multimodal foundation models such as SkyReels-V4 [69], Seedance 2.0 [51], and Veo 3.1 [120] already support image-conditioned video generation with audio or sound. Benchmarks. As summarized in Table 14, VoxCeleb/VoxCeleb2 [109, 416], LRS2/LRS3 [3, 498], HDTF [732], and CelebV-HQ [765] remain standard for identity preservation, speech synchronization, and portrait quality. Recent resources broaden the scope: TalkVid-Bench [85] target more diverse talking-head training and evaluation, TalkVerse [590] emphasizes minute-long generation, TalkCuts [72] targets multi-shot speech videos, MIT [769] benchmarks multi-human interaction, and THEval [447] and EvalTalker [756] provide finer-grained perceptual evaluation. 6.2.4 Audio-Driven 3D Visual Generation Definition. Audio-driven 3D visual generation maps sound into dynamic 3D facial, head, or body representations V3D(t). Relative to 2D animation, the 3D formulation offers viewpoint consistency, explicit geometry, and better compatibility with downstream rendering, but it also makes subtle motion realism harder to learn. Representative Tasks and Methods. Audio-driven 3D synthesis spans five recurring directions. 3D Face Animation & Talking Heads deform explicit mesh topologies such as FLAME or SMPL. Early regression systems (VOCA [113], MeshTalk [463]) often over-smoothed motion, whereas FaceFormer [144], CodeTalker [633], and StreamingTalker [661] improve co-articulation and streaming behavior through stronger sequence modeling. Neural Rendering & Dynamic Avatars focus on photorealism. AD-NeRF [194] pioneered audio-conditioned NeRFs, GeneFace [673] improved cross-identity transfer, and recent 3D Gaussian Splatting systems such as GaussianTalker [685] and SynGauss [758] make interactive rendering increasingly practical. Emotion & Expression Enhancement explicitly combats dead-face artifacts. EVP [246], DreamTalk [383], and EmoGene [571] disentangle content from style or emotion so that non-verbal dynamics are not washed out by phoneme tracking alone. Co-speech Gestures & Full-Body Motion map speech to skeletal kinematics. TalkSHOW [675] separates facial and bodily channels, while EMAGE [342] improves gesture diversity and beat consistency with masked gesture transformers and compositional VQ-VAEs. 36 Table 15 Representative datasets for audio-driven 3D visual generation. Dataset Modality Hours Focus VoxCeleb2 [109] MEAD [560] HDTF [732] VOCASET [113] BEAT2 [342] Face (2D video) Face (2D video) Face (2D video) Face (3D mesh) Full body (3D) 2,442 40 15.8 0.48 60 In-the-wild pretraining and identity robustness Multi-view emotion control High-resolution talking-head rendering Registered 4D scans and mesh-level evaluation Holistic gesture/body generation with semantics Holistic Human Synthesis targets full 3D humans. Stereo-Talker [126] and Real3D-Portrait [674] move beyond the head-only regime by coupling portrait reconstruction with coordinated body and background dynamics. Benchmarks. Benchmarks depend on the output representation: VoxCeleb2 [109] and HDTF [732] support portrait synthesis, MEAD [560] adds controlled emotion, VOCASET [113] and BIWI serve mesh-level evaluation, and BEAT2 [342] targets holistic gesture generation. Metrics span geometric accuracy (LVE, vertex error), lip-sync quality (LSE-C/LSE-D), perceptual realism (FID), and body-motion alignment such as Beat Constancy or FGD [342].

sections.title: 6.3 Joint Audio-Visual Generation
sections.content: Joint generation optimizes for coupled audio and video (or related edits) in single objective or training recipe, rather than stapling unimodal output to another model as an afterthought. The following subsections cover textand image-conditioned joint synthesis as well as joint audio-video editing workflows. 6.3.1 Text-to-Audio-Video Generation Definition. Text-to-audio-video (T2AV) generation synthesizes both video and synchronized audio from textual prompt [355]. strong model must satisfy three conditions at once: within-modality quality, faithfulness to the prompt, and cross-modal synchronization. Methods. MM-Diffusion [469] first showed joint audio-video diffusion, but only in narrow domains such as landscapes and dance [291, 312]. The text-conditional era begins with JavisDiT [355], after which several design lines emerge. Architecture Evolution. Early attempts such as JavisDiT [355], UniVerse-1 [552], BridgeDiT [189], and Ovi [369] keep audio and video branches partially separate while exchanging information through specific synchronization modules (e.g., cross-attention). Recent approaches including JoVA [233] and JavisDiT++ [356] move toward more unified modeling by shared or tightly coupled attention blocks to improve cross-modal coherence. Audio-Visual Synchronization. Commonly-used strategies includes text-derived temporal priors [355], framelevel cross-attention [220, 369, 552, 736], or aligned RoPE position IDs [233, 356] keep both modalities in step. On the other hand, data scaling [684] also plays vital role in achieving high-quality synchronization. Multi-Task Unification. Beyond the standalone text-to-audio-video (T2AV) generation, recent works (e.g., Harmony [220], UniAVGen [711], and Apollo [555]) widely explore multi-task integration (e.g., A2V, V2A, TI2AV, etc.) to achieve better modality learning and cross-task synergy, and OmniForcing [501] further extends to real-time joint audio-video generation. Post-Training Application. Preference optimization via DPO [351] or GRPO [350] is also becoming standard for improving prompt following and subjective quality [87]. For instance, JavisDiT++ [356] derives the AV-DPO strategy to investigate DPO adaptation to joint audio-video generation. Agentic Workflow. In contrast to end-to-end T2AV models, agentic approaches decompose audio-visual generation into cascaded stages, typically text-to-video plus video-to-audio. MV-Crafter [68] is an early workflow-style example, while ReelWave [596], LVAS-Agent [727], and AutoMV [515] more explicitly formulate 37 Table 16 Representative datasets and curated evaluation suites for text-to-audio-video generation. Name Greatest Hits [427] Landscape [291] VGGSound [70] AVSync15 [715] JavisBench [355] Verse-Bench [552] Harmony-Bench [220] VABench [222] T2AV-Compass [55] PhyAVBench [629] Type Dataset Dataset Dataset Suite Suite Suite Suite Suite Suite Suite Scale Audio Primary Focus 977 videos 928 videos 200K clips 150 clips 10,140 prompts 600 prompts 150 prompts 778 prompts 500 prompts 1,000 prompts Sound Sound Sound Mixed Sound Sound Speech Sound Speech Sound Music Speech Sound Music Speech Sound Music Speech Impact/onset sync Ambient scene realism Open-domain coverage Fine-grained AV sync Quality, semantics, sync Speech-inclusive T2AV Prompt following; sync Broad audio coverage Unified multimodal scoring Physical/causal grounding Table 17 Performance comparison of T2AV models on T2AV-Compass [55]. Best results in bold. Model Open-Source Video Quality Audio Quality VT CU PQ VA Cross-modal Alignment A-V T-A T-V DS LS Veo-3.1 [183] Sora-2 [424] Kling-2.6 [282] Wan-2.6 [12] Seedance-1.5 [87] PixVerse-V5.5 [438] Ovi-1.1 [369] JavisDiT [355] 13.39 7.568 11.41 11.87 12.74 11.54 9.336 6.850 5.425 4.112 5.417 4.605 5.007 4.558 4.368 3.575 7.015 5.827 6.882 6.658 7.555 6.108 6.569 4.299 6.621 5.340 6.449 6.222 7.250 5.855 6.492 5. 0.2335 0.2438 0.6776 1.509 0.2856 0.2484 0.2432 0.8100 1.331 0.2419 0.2495 0.2449 0.7852 1.502 0.2495 0.2149 0.2572 0.2451 0.8818 1.081 0.2875 0.2320 0.2370 0.8650 1.560 0.2305 0.2431 0.6627 1.306 0.1816 0.1756 0.2391 0.9624 1.191 0.1620 0.1257 0.2320 1.3220 0.1284 - soundtrack or music-video generation as multi-agent pipelines. Related systems such as AesopAgent [553], MM-StoryAgent [646], MovieAgent [616], AniME [717], and MAViS [565] further support the value of explicit planning for long-form generation. Nowadays, commercial systems (including Sora2, Veo3.1 [120], Kling3.0, Wan2.6, and Seedance 2.0 Pro) are still ahead because they combine larger audio-video corpora, stronger base generators, and heavier posttraining. On the other hand, open-source models are catching up with proprietary systems in performance, where LTX-2 [200] and MOVA [684] demonstrate promising potential and scalability. Open Problem: Scale Still Decides T2AV currently has the widest gap between public research prototypes and frontier product systems. The difference is not single architectural trick; it is data scale, base-model maturity, and post-training depth combined. Open models have largely solved the question of how to build joint AV generators, but not yet the question of how to train them at product-level scale. Benchmarks. As shown in Table 16, early benchmarks (Greatest Hits [427], Landscape [291], VGGSound [70], AVSync15 [715]) cover limited scenarios without standardized metrics. Curated suites such as JavisBench [355], Verse-Bench [552], Harmony-Bench [220], VABench [222], and T2AV-Compass [55] provide broader prompt coverage and more systematic scoring, while PhyAVBench [629] explicitly measures physical plausibility. The evaluation trend is moving from modality-specific metrics toward composite scores that jointly assess prompt following, perceptual quality, and temporal coherence. 38 6.3.2 Image-to-Audio-Video Generation Definition. Image-to-audio-video (I2AV) generation turns single image into sounding video clip. Unlike T2AV, appearance is anchored by the reference image, while motion and sound must be inferred jointly under much tighter spatial constraint. Methods. Early I2AV solutions are mostly cascaded. Independent generation combines an image-to-video model such as Stable Video Diffusion [43] with an image-to-audio model such as IM2Wav [482], but provides weak audio-video coupling. Video-first pipelines animate the image and then add sound with video-to-audio models such as Diff-Foley [374] and FoleyCrafter [729], while caption-mediated variants rely on image-derived prompts for separate video and audio generators [218, 278, 344]. dedicated line emerged with Animate and Sound an Image [573], which explicitly formulates image-tosounding-video generation with joint transformer conditioning. Recent unified AV models further absorb image-conditioned generation into single framework, including UniVerse-1 [552], Ovi [369], Klear [555], ALIVE [193], and SkyReels-V4 [69]. The same trend appears in production systems such as Kling Video 2.6/3.0/3.0 Omni [283], Wan2.6 [548], Seedance 1.5 pro/2.0 [51, 87], Vidu Q3 [544], Sora 2 [422], Veo 3.1 [120], and Runway Gen-4.5 [470], which all expose image-conditioned or reference-guided audio-video generation in an integrated workflow. Benchmarks. Existing benchmarks for image-conditional I2AV generation remain scarce. AVSync15 [715] is the most used benchmark for synchronized sounding-video generation, and Verse-Bench [552] further supports image-conditioned joint audio-video evaluation. More standardized benchmarks are still needed for this setting. 6.3.3 Joint Audio-Video Editing Definition. Joint audio-video editing edits video and sound together so that the result remains synchronized and semantically consistent across modalities. Given (V, A) and an edit condition c, the model produces (V , A) that applies the intended change to both streams while preserving alignment and minimizing changes to unrelated content. Representative Tasks and Methods. Early work, such as one-shot adaptation [327] and AvED [335], establishes instruction-based and zero-shot joint editing, mainly targeting prompt-consistent changes without strong paired supervision. Meanwhile, more recent methods move to finer granularity. Object-AVEdit [156] performs object-level addition, replacement, and removal. VAInpaint [611] addresses zero-shot joint inpainting and removal with visual masks and LLM-assisted audio querying. SAVE [644] formulates paired source-to-target audiovisual removal, while AVI-Edit [744] further supports instance-level masked editing with refined spatial control. For specialized settings, Coherent Audio-Visual Editing [240] edits audio conditionally after video edits, and JUST-DUB-IT [63] adapts joint AV diffusion for dubbing-style speech and facial-motion editing. broader recent trend is to absorb editing into unified joint AV backbones rather than treating it as standalone task. UniAVGen [711] and SkyReels-V4 [69] suggest this direction by supporting continuation, inpainting, and editing-like workflows within shared audio-video generation framework. Benchmarks. Dedicated joint AV editing benchmarks are still limited, but they have evolved from small prompt-based sets to large-scale mask-aware and paired-data benchmarks. As organized in Table 18, OAVE [327] is an early benchmark for one-shot joint editing, and AvED-Bench [335] evaluates full-video zero-shot editing. More recently, AVISet [744] and SAVEBench [644] provide instance masks or paired source-target supervision, enabling evaluation of edit faithfulness, preservation, and synchronization under localized edits. 39 Table 18 Representative benchmarks for joint audio-video editing. Dataset OAVE [327] AvED-Bench [335] AVISet [744] SAVEBench [644] Scale 44 events 110 videos 73k clips 17k pairs Edit Scope Key Feature One-shot event editing Full-video editing Instance-level editing Object-level removal Early dedicated benchmark Zero-shot prompt-based evaluation Masks and paired edit instructions Paired source-target supervision Trend: From Joint Editing to Precise Intervention The field is shifting from coarse joint modification to precise cross-modal intervention. The real challenge is not just editing audio and video together, but doing so locally, synchronously, and with minimal side effects. This trend is pushing joint AV editing toward finer-grained supervision and unified AV foundation models.

sections.title: 7 Audio-Visual Interaction
sections.content: Audio-visual interaction concerns systems that do not merely perceive multimodal content, but must respond to it in real time. We distinguish two settings: conversational interaction, where the response is language or speech, and embodied interaction, where the response is an action in the physical world. Both require tightly coupled perception, memory, reasoning, and generation under latency constraints, making interaction stricter test of AV intelligence than static understanding alone.

sections.title: 7.1 Interactive Audio-Visual Conversation
sections.content: Conversational AV systems aim for natural multi-turn interaction over speech, images, video, and text. We organize this subsection around three stages of conversational capability: speech-first interaction, unified visual understanding and generation, and omni-modal conversation that handles arbitrary input/output combinations, as summarized in Figure 10. The persistent constraints are latency, context retention, and the preservation of non-verbal cues such as prosody, emotion, and temporal grounding. 7.1.1 Audio-Driven Conversation Definition. Audio-driven conversation refers to dialogue systems that take speech, environmental sound, or music as conversational input and respond in text or speech. Unlike text-only chat, these systems must preserve acoustic content, linguistic semantics, and paralinguistic cues such as prosody, emotion, and speaker identity. Recent AudioLLMs [244] make this formulation practical for assistants, agents, and streaming multimodal interaction. Methods. Current systems under this category can be organized by how they pass acoustic information through the dialogue loop: Cascaded ASRLLMTTS pipelines explicitly transcribe speech, reason in text, and synthesize speech again, as in AudioGPT [231]. Cascades are modular and easy to debug, but they accumulate latency and lose prosodic information at the text bottleneck. Continuous-feature audio-to-text models feed speech or audio representations into an LLM and usually return text. They use pretrained encoders such as Wav2Vec [477], HuBERT [219], Whisper [451], or WavLM [82], with representative systems including Qwen-Audio/Qwen2-Audio [106, 107], SpeechVerse [119], and E-Chat [648]. Discrete-token and end-to-end speech-to-speech models represent speech with semantic or codec tokens and generate spoken responses directly or through vocoder [138]. SpeechGPT [708], Mini-Omni [631], LLaMA40 Figure 10 Organization of interactive audio-visual conversation. We organize this subsection into audio-driven speech interaction, unified visual understanding and generation, and omni-modal conversation for real-time any-to-any multimodal exchange. Omni [145], Moshi [122], IntrinsicVoice [724], OmniFlatten [721], Freeze-Omni [577], and PSLM [409] are representative of this direction. Acoustic-oriented and cross-domain audio models try to preserve non-linguistic information such as emotion, timbre, speaker identity, and environmental sound. Some models use unified encoders for speech, sound, and music, such as Audio Flamingo [279], Audio Flamingo 2 [175], and Qwen-Audio [106]; others use multiple domain-specific encoders or projectors, such as SALMONN [514] and GAMA [174]. LauraGPT [137] and SpeechGPT-Gen [709] further show how audio regeneration can preserve richer acoustic detail. Trend: Speech Drops the Cascade Speech interaction is moving away from hard ASRLLMTTS decomposition, but cascades are not obsolete. End-to-end systems are better at latency and prosody preservation, whereas cascades still win on modularity and controllable intermediate text. The most practical near-term compromise is token-based hybrids that keep more acoustic detail without giving up LLM-compatible interfaces. Benchmarks. Evaluation is still fragmented across sound, music, and speech. Domain-specific suites include Clotho-AQA [338] and AudioCaps [265] for environmental sounds, MuChoMusic [598], MusicBench [402], and CMI-Bench [385] for music, and LibriSQA [741] plus Dynamic-SUPERB [226] for speech. More recent cross-domain suites such as AudioBench [550], AIR-Bench [657], MMAU [475], and MMAR [388] better reflect the breadth expected from conversational audio models. 7.1.2 Unified Visual Understanding and Generation Definition. Unified visual understanding and generation seeks one model that can both interpret and synthesize images or videos. The appeal is architectural consistency: shared representation can support perception, editing, and generation within single conversational loop. Representative Tasks and Methods. We discuss unified image and video modeling separately. Table 19 Representative evaluation suites relevant to audio-driven conversation and audio-input dialogue models. Cross-domain denotes benchmarks that intentionally mix multiple audio domains or reasoning settings. Dataset Sound Music Speech Cross-domain Primary Focus Clotho-AQA [338] AudioCaps [265] MuChoMusic [598] MusicBench [402] CMI-Bench [385] LibriSQA [741] Dynamic-SUPERB [226] AudioBench [550] AIR-Bench [657] MMAU [475] MMAR [388] Audio question answering Grounded audio captioning Music understanding Music instruction following Comprehensive music understanding Spoken question answering Robust speech interaction Broad audio understanding Instruction following and reasoning Multiple-choice audio understanding Multi-step audio reasoning Unified image understanding and generation. Recent image models can be grouped by where understanding and generation are coupled: (1) MLLM-to-diffusion bridging. This line keeps an MLLM as the semantic core and links it to diffusion or DiT-style generator through an explicit interface. Representative examples include MetaQuery [429], BLIP3o-NEXT [75], and Skywork UniPic 2.0 [600]. These models retain strong reasoning and generation modules, but the interface can limit deeper transfer. (2) Block-level fusion of MLLM and diffusion-style generation modules. second family couples understanding and generation more tightly by sharing transformer computation rather than using only lightweight connector. BAGEL [123], JanusFlow [386], and Show-o2 [628] are representative examples. (3) Native all-modal next-token autoregression. third line maps all modalities into shared token space and trains them with next-token prediction. Earlier representatives such as Chameleon [518] and Janus-Pro [90] established this route. Recent work, including SelfTok [551], Ming-UniVision [237], and LongCat-Next [622], further strengthens it with improved tokenization and interleaved multimodal generation. (4) Text autoregression with visual masked autoregression. Another line keeps causal autoregression for text but adopts masked autoregressive modeling for images. Harmon [615] is representative example, and Skywork UniPic 1.0 [563] is closely related. This design keeps unified semantic framework without forcing all modalities to share the same decoding rule. (5) Masked discrete diffusion. newer line replaces autoregression with mask-based discrete diffusion. Omni-Diffusion [308] is the clearest representative, while LLaDA-o [682] further develops this direction by combining discrete masked diffusion for understanding with continuous diffusion for visual generation. Unified video understanding and generation. Compared with the image side, unified video modeling remains less diverse and is still dominated by hybrid designs. Most current systems pair an MLLM with dedicated video generator, as in Omni-Video [513], UniVid [373], and UniVideo [599]. Tokenizer-centric work such as Divot [167] explores shared video representations for both comprehension and generation, while Uni-ViGU [443] moves toward generation-centric design. Overall, unified video modeling is still at an early stage. Benchmarks. Image understanding is commonly tested on COCO Captioning [92], NoCaps [7], VQAv2 [186], GQA [238], ScienceQA [474], MMBench [364], and SEED-Bench [297], while image generation uses MSCOCO [331], DrawBench [473], and GenEval [172]. Video understanding uses MSVD-QA [636], ActivityNetQA [695], and TVQA [293]; video generation is typically measured on WebVid [33], MSR-VTT [640], and VBench [235]. What is still lacking is benchmark that jointly punishes failures in both understanding and generation over long visual contexts. 42 Key Difference: Quality Still Favors Hybrids The trade-off is straightforward: autoregressive unification offers conceptual simplicity, whereas hybrid systems offer better visual quality. In practice, most high-performing systems now accept extra complexity because the quality gap remains large, especially for video. promising middle ground is emerging around continuous or latent-token autoregression, which keeps the single-model view while relaxing the worst bottlenecks of discrete visual tokenization. 7.1.3 Omni-Modal Audio-Visual Conversation Definition. Omni-modal audio-visual conversation concerns dialogue systems that jointly ground responses in both what the model sees and what it hears, while returning text or, increasingly, streaming speech. Compared with video chat models that answer in text only, the target here is end-to-end or tightly integrated audiovisual interaction, often under low-latency, multi-turn, and real-time constraints. Methods. Recent work in this area follows two converging lines. The first line focuses on omni assistants for real-time audio-visual dialogue with speech output. Early open efforts moved from the speech-centric Mini-Omni [631] to the visual-audio Mini-Omni2 [632], and then to stronger vision-speech systems such as VITA-1.5 [154], InteractiveOmni [530], and Baichuan-Omni1.5 [320]. More recent omni models, including GPT-4o [239], Gemini 3.1 Flash [184], Qwen2.5-Omni [638], Qwen3-Omni [639], Ming-Omni [8], and LongCat-Flash-Omni [549], further unify video, audio, and speech generation within single conversational stack. Architecturally, these systems usually combine visual and audio encoders with an LLM backbone and streaming speech decoder, with representative designs such as block-wise streaming encoders, interleaved audio-video tokenization, and Thinker-Talker style decoupling between reasoning and speech synthesis. The second line studies unified understanding-generation models, where conversation is treated as one instance of broader any-to-any multimodal interaction. MultiDialog [433] introduced an early direct face-to-face dialogue model that maps audio-visual speech to audio-visual speech without relying on intermediate text. VideoPoet [275], NExT-GPT [613], and Unified-IO 2 [370] broadened this agenda by leveraging pretrained components or autoregressively modeling multimodal inputs and outputs across video, audio, image, and text, making generation and comprehension part of one framework. More recent systems, such as JavisGPT [354] and X-Streamer [630], move closer to genuine omni-modal audio-visual conversation by coupling sounding-video comprehension with synchronized audiovisual generation and real-time interaction. AR-Omni [100] is also relevant as fully autoregressive bridge toward unified multimodal generation, although its current formulation mainly centers on text, image, and speech rather than strict video-grounded dialogue. Benchmarks. Naturalistic AV dialogue data is still scarce. MultiDialog [433] provides 340 hours of face-to-face conversation with aligned video, audio, and affective annotations, while AVInstruct [670] and Video-ChatGPT [390] expand instruction-style supervision. For evaluation, Video-MME [153] probes longvideo reasoning with audio transcripts, and OmniVideoBench [298] targets omni-modal AV understanding more explicitly. Even with these additions, open-ended multi-turn AV dialogue remains under-benchmarked. Very recently, AVI-Bench [583] categorizes the evaluation of audio-visual tasks into three stages: perception, understanding, and reasoning, inspired by human perceptual processes. 43 Figure 11 Organization of audio-visual embodied interaction. We organize this subsection into navigation, scene understanding, and manipulation, grounded in the shared foundations of audio-visual perception, cross-modal alignment, and embodied reasoning. Open Problem: Real Dialogue Is Missing The biggest bottleneck is still data, not architecture. Synthetic instruction data scales, but it rarely captures authentic conversational timing, grounded audio events, or the spontaneity of human-human interaction. Until naturalistic AV dialogue corpora become much larger, omni-modal conversation will likely continue to underperform its architectural promise.

sections.title: 7.2 Interactive Audio-Visual Embodiment
sections.content: Embodied AV intelligence grounds multimodal perception in action. We organize this subsection around three abilities: navigating toward sound sources, reconstructing environments from visual and acoustic cues, and manipulating objects using contact sounds as feedback, as summarized in Figure 11. Across all three, the key challenge is turning multimodal understanding into control policies that remain robust outside clean simulation. 7.2.1 Audio-Visual Navigation Definition. Audio-visual navigation (AVN) asks an embodied agent to reach sound-emitting target by jointly using what it sees and hears. Sound complements vision in two ways: it exposes out-of-view goals and carries structural cues through reverberation and propagation. The standard task variants are AudioGoal, where the sound itself defines the target, and AudioPointGoal, where coarse directional hint is also provided [64]. Methods. AVN has evolved through several complementary lines. 1) End-to-end RL methods [64, 66, 74, 303] fuse visual and binaural features and learn recurrent navigation policies directly from reward. Memoryaugmented and hierarchical methods [66, 158, 316] introduce waypoint planning, spatial memory, or transformer recurrence to reduce myopic behavior. 2) Semantic navigation [65] shifts the task from tracking an active sound source to reasoning about semantic sound events whose source may become silent. Robust navigation further addresses noise, moving targets, and adversarial conditions through dynamic modality weighting, stereo-aware fusion, and explicit robustness objectives [303, 490, 586, 683, 693, 740]. 3) Language-augmented 44 systems decouple perception from planning. AVLEN [435] introduces language queries, and RILA-style agents [363, 663] use higher-level reasoning and self-correction to improve zero-shot generalization. 4) Sim-to-real and acoustic-field modeling address the gap between clean simulation and physical deployment. SoundSpaces 2.0 [67] improves continuous acoustic rendering, RAF [98] provides real dense room impulse responses, Sonicverse [163] supports multisensory embodied simulation, and noisy navigation settings such as BeDAViN [490] stress robustness under more realistic sound conditions. Benchmarks. SoundSpaces [64] provides geometric acoustic simulation over Replica and Matterport3D scenes; its PanoIR extension adds panoramic impulse responses across 100+ scenes; and SoundSpaces 2.0 [67] supports continuous acoustic rendering on arbitrary 3D meshes. Real Acoustic Fields (RAF) [98] offers real-world dense RIRs with multi-view images and 6DoF pose tracking. Sonicverse [163] is multimodal simulation platform for embodied AV tasks, and BeDAViN [490] extends standard benchmarks with realistic noise conditions. Table 20 Summary of AVN benchmarks. Dataset #Scenes Resolution Sampling Rate Realworld Avg. Area #Training Episodes #Test Episodes Replica [64] Matterport3D [64] RAF [98] Sonicverse [163] SoundSpaces 2.0 [67] BeDAViN [490] 18 85 2 103 103 85 0.5m 1m Continuous Continuous Continuous 1m 44100Hz 16000Hz 48000Hz 44100Hz 16000Hz 96000Hz 47.24m2 517.34m2 45.1m2 215.3m2 435.4m2 517.3m2 0.1M 2M 3000 5M 10M 1.5M 1000 1000 500 1000 1000 3000 Open Problem: Agents Need Grounding AVN is shifting from reactive control toward deliberative control. LLM-based agents bring stronger priors about rooms, objects, and human goals, which improves semantic generalization, but they are only useful when tightly grounded in low-latency sensory feedback. The next gains will likely come from better interfaces between symbolic planning and continuous audio-visual control, not from larger language models alone. 7.2.2 Audio-Visual Scene Understanding and Reconstruction Definition. In the embodied setting, audio-visual scene understanding and reconstruction aim to build an actionable spatial representation for an agent rather than purely descriptive scene label. The goal is to estimate geometry, acoustic response, and language-grounded semantics tightly enough that robot can localize sound sources, reason about out-of-view structure, and update its spatial memory while acting [67, 372]. This is therefore narrower than generic AV segmentation or event classification: the output must be useful for navigation, manipulation, or world modeling. Representative Tasks and Methods. Related approaches can be categorized as follows: Echo-based geometry and acoustic reconstruction. Early work such as BatVision, Beyond2D, and few-shot acoustic reconstruction [105, 392, 432] showed that echoes can recover layout cues when vision is degraded. In embodied AV systems, this line has evolved into queryable acoustic fields: SoundSpaces 2.0 [67] provides the simulation substrate, and Neural Acoustic Fields (NAF) [372] represent room acoustics as continuous functions that agents can query for localization, reverberation, or planning. Joint visual-acoustic scene fields. Recent work explicitly couples geometry, appearance, and acoustics in one scene representation. AV-NeRF [326], AV-GS [38], NeRAF [50], and related audio-aware neural field models move toward physically grounded visual-acoustic rendering. The important embodied point is not Table 21 Audio-visual navigation performance on SoundSpaces [64]. Top: AudioGoal (continuous sound) on Replica (Heard). Bottom: Semantic AudioGoal (intermittent sound) on MP3D (Unheard). The two task settings differ substantially in difficulty. SPL and SR are in %. : zero-shot (no training trajectories). Best per-task results in bold. Method Paradigm SPL AudioGoal: Replica (Heard) SoundSpaces [64] AV-WaN [66] End-to-End RL Waypoint Nav Semantic AudioGoal: MP3D (Unheard) SoundSpaces [64] AV-WaN [66] SAVi [65] AVLEN [435] RILA [663] End-to-End RL Waypoint Nav Semantic Nav Language-Aug LLM Agent 74.4 86. 15.5 13.2 17.2 17.6 11.8 SR 91.4 98.7 16.5 17.2 24.8 26.2 35. Table 22 Representative resources and evaluation settings for audio-visual scene understanding and reconstruction. The emphasis is on spatial memory and field estimation rather than generic AV perception. Resource Signal Use Metrics SoundSpaces 2.0 [67] RAF [98] SonicVerse [163] AVLMaps / MSLMaps [224, 225] 3D scans + sim audio Real RIRs + RGB Sim AV rooms AV-lang maps AV sim Acoustic recon Sim2real Grounded QA SPL/SR + loc err RT60 + loc/recon err Success + acoustic err Success + grounding photorealism by itself, but whether the learned field stays spatially consistent enough to support downstream decisions under viewpoint change. Language-grounded spatial memory. Audio-Visual Language Maps [224] make this actionability explicit by grounding language, vision, and audio in shared 3D frame that can be queried during navigation. The extension to Multimodal Spatial Language Maps [225] broadens the same map abstraction from navigation toward manipulation, suggesting that embodied AV scene understanding is converging on reusable spatial memory rather than task-specific perception heads. Toward interactive AV world models. The frontier is shifting from static maps to updateable world models. X-Streamer [630] is an example of unified audiovisual world modeling, and even though it is not limited to robot mapping, it points to the next step for embodiment: scene representations that evolve with interaction, preserve long-horizon audiovisual memory, and expose state variables useful for control. Benchmarks. Embodied evaluation should reflect spatial usefulness rather than generic AV recognition. The most relevant resources are therefore simulators, acoustic-field datasets, and map-based evaluation settings: SoundSpaces 2.0 [67] and SonicVerse [163] support embodied simulation, RAF [98] measures real acoustic reconstruction, and language-grounded map settings such as AVLMaps/MSLMaps [224, 225] test whether the representation can support embodied queries and downstream success. Typical metrics include localization error, RT60 or field-reconstruction error, success rate, and task-completion metrics on navigation/manipulation splits. 46 Table 23 Representative benchmarks for audio-visual embodied manipulation. Benchmark Setting Modalities Scale Primary Use ManiWAV [366] ARIO [591] Kaiwu [248] Audio-VLA [602] Real Real Sim Real Sim In-wild demos RGB, audio, state Multimodal robot traj. Millions episodes Audio, video, mocap RGB, audio, action Assembly data RLBench/LIBERO Contact manipulation Embodied pretraining Industrial manipulation TCR process eval Key Difference: State Must Guide Action This subsection should not collapse into sounding-object segmentation or generic AV scene parsing. For embodiment, the useful representation is the one that helps an agent decide where to move, what is out of view, and how the environment will sound after an action. That is why map-like spatial memory, queryable acoustic fields, and world-model interfaces matter more here than standalone AV classification accuracy. 7.2.3 Audio-Visual Embodiment Interaction and Manipulation Definition. Audio-visual embodiment interaction and manipulation study how agents use sound together with vision for contact-rich control. Contact sounds reveal events such as slip, collision, pouring progress, or material change that are often ambiguous in RGB alone, making audio useful proxy for tactile feedback. Acoustic sensing can be passive, where the agent listens to naturally occurring contact sounds, or active, where the agent emits vibrations or probing signals and interprets the returned acoustic response. Representative Tasks and Methods. Research has evolved through four related directions. Contact representation and feature enhancement [301, 401, 585] use audio to enrich visual perception, especially for contact events that are visually subtle. Active and passive acoustic sensing studies how contact sounds or controlled acoustic probing reveal object state, material, and interaction dynamics; SonicSense [349] is representative of in-hand acoustic object perception. World-model and generative approaches [236, 566, 678, 710] integrate audio into diffusion policies or audio-centric predictive models so that agents can anticipate physical interactions. VLA integration [591, 602, 738] injects auditory tokens directly into visionlanguageaction backbones for closed-loop multimodal control. Benchmarks. Benchmarks are now appearing across both real and simulated settings. ManiWAV [366] collects in-the-wild AV demonstrations through an ear-in-hand setup, ARIO [591] aggregates multi-robot trajectories into unified format, Kaiwu [248] targets real-world assembly with rich synchronized sensing, and AV grounding-and-act [585] stresses perception-action coupling. Audio-VLA [602] augments RLBench and LIBERO with collision audio and introduces Task Completion Rate (TCR) for dynamic process tracking, while sim-to-real evaluation increasingly uses intentionally hard-to-simulate audio [566]. Open Problem: Simulation Still Misleads Sim-to-real transfer remains the defining obstacle for embodied AV systems. Visual simulation is already imperfect; acoustic simulation is usually worse, especially for contact-rich manipulation where tiny sound differences signal large changes in task state. Progress will depend on better acoustic rendering, more domain-invariant representations, and training recipes that use simulation for scale without overfitting to its unrealistic sound statistics. 47 Figure 12 The landscape of AVI applications. We here highlight how audio-visual foundation models act as core intelligence layer for AIGC, human-centric services, immersive environments, and ubiquitous perception, etc.

sections.title: 8 Applications
sections.content: Audio-visual intelligence powered by foundation models has enabled wide range of real-world applications spanning creative industries, human-computer interaction, immersive technologies, and embodied systems. This section surveys the major domains where these advances have shown substantial impact.

sections.title: 8.1 AIGC and Creative Content Production
sections.content: Generative tools are already changing how video and sound are produced, from automated Foley and shortform co-creation to long-standing workflows such as post-production, music scoring, and asset iteration. The discussion below links foundation-model capabilities in generation and editing to these creative pipelines, using film and game-oriented examples before broadening to music and sound design. Film and Video Post-Production. The film and video production industry has been transformed by foundation models capable of generating and manipulating audio-visual content [355, 469]. Foley synthesis, traditionally reliant on manual labor and specialized studios, can now be automated by video-to-audio models such as Diff-Foley [374], MMAudio [101], and FoleyCrafter [729], which generate temporally aligned sound effects and ambient audio directly from silent video, reducing post-production time and cost. These models also support dubbing and voice-over workflows: speech-driven talking head generation [442, 673] enables crosslingual lip-synchronized dubbing, while voice conversion systems [440] preserve emotional nuance during voice transformation. More recently, joint text-to-audio-video generation models such as JavisDiT [355] and commercial systems including Veo-3 and Seedance [87] have opened the possibility of generating complete scenes with synchronized audio and visuals from text, though current performance is still strongest for short-form content. Music and Sound Design. Foundation models have also reshaped music production and sound design workflows [6, 46, 112]. Text-to-audio systems such as AudioLDM [343], MusicGen [112], and Stable Audio allow creators to rapidly prototype audio from natural language prompts, while source separation models including TF-GridNet [592] and Band-Split RNN [376] can isolate instruments from mixed recordings for karaoke generation, remixing, and restoration. These capabilities are increasingly integrated into digital audio 48 workstations and video editing tools [281]. In addition, audio manipulation models support style transfer, inpainting of corrupted segments [412], and instruction-based editing [588]. In gaming, procedural sound generation conditioned on game state and visual context reduces dependence on large prerecorded libraries while enabling more dynamic and context-aware audio experiences.

sections.title: 8.2 Digital Humans and Social Interaction
sections.content: Synthesized faces, bodies, and consistent personas sit at the boundary of entertainment, communication, and service interfaces; audio-driven motion and identity-locked output are the usual technical levers. We first sketch avatar and talking-head technology, then comment on applications that require the same person (or character) to persist across sessions and media. Talking Head and Avatars. Digital human synthesis has progressed from simple 2D lip-syncing to photorealistic 3D avatar generation [113, 463]. Early methods such as Wav2Lip [442] focused on robust audio-lip synchronization at the pixel level, whereas newer approaches use explicit 3D representations. Mesh-based methods such as FaceFormer [144] and CodeTalker [633] animate parametric face models, while neural rendering approaches, including AD-NeRF [194] and GaussianTalker [685], achieve photorealistic synthesis through implicit representations. Recent work further addresses the dead-face problem [246] by separating emotional state from speech content. Models such as DreamTalk [383] and EmoGene [571] improve expressive control, and full-body systems such as EMAGE [342] and Stereo-Talker [126] generate synchronized speech, facial expressions, and co-speech gestures from single reference image. Persona-Consistent Interaction. Maintaining identity consistency across interactions is essential for virtual influencers, customer service avatars, and personalized AI companions [188, 675]. Foundation models increasingly support one-shot or few-shot avatar creation, where small set of reference images or videos establishes identity priors that persist across generated content. Real3D-Portrait [674] reconstructs animatable 3D portraits from single image, while identity-preserving video generation maintains consistent appearance across varying poses and expressions [483]. These capabilities enable applications such as virtual influencer marketing, realistic telepresence avatars [578], and sign language synthesis for accessibility. Cross-lingual voice conversion and lip-sync adaptation further expand digital human applications to multilingual settings [732].

sections.title: 8.3 Human-Centric Intelligent Services
sections.content: End-user and enterprise settings reward systems that can listen, watch, and speak on human timescales: meetings, education, and accessibility are representative stress tests for robust multimodal input and output. Assistants and meeting analytics, learning and training, and inclusive design illustrate that breadth without duplicating the technical review earlier in the survey. Conversational Assistants and Meeting Intelligence. Audio LLMs such as Qwen-Audio [106, 107] and SALMONN [514] expand conversational AI from text to native audio understanding. By processing speech directly without intermediate ASR transcription, they preserve paralinguistic cues such as emotion, emphasis, and speaker identity [127, 174]. Omni models such as GPT-4o [239] and Qwen-Omni [638, 639] further integrate real-time audio-visual perception and generation, enabling more natural multimodal dialogue [530]. In parallel, meeting intelligence systems use audio-visual understanding for speaker diarization, action item extraction, and conference summarization [109, 416]. Multimodal fusion improves speaker identification by combining vocal and facial cues [128], while emotion recognition supports analysis of meeting dynamics in applications such as collaboration analytics and customer-facing services [387]. Education and Training. Interactive educational content benefits from both audio-visual generation and understanding [306, 346]. AI tutors can adapt presentation styles according to learner engagement inferred from audio-visual signals [617], while generated explanatory videos with synchronized narration and demonstrations support self-paced learning. Language learning applications further use lip-sync generation [442] and audiodriven avatar teachers [144] to demonstrate pronunciation and articulatory movements. In high-stakes training domains such as medicine, aviation, and emergency response, audio-visual generation can produce realistic 49 scenarios without expensive physical infrastructure [67, 163]. Audio-visual analysis also enables automated feedback on communication, procedural performance, and situational awareness [451]. Accessibility and Inclusive AI. Audio-visual foundation models significantly improve accessibility for users with sensory impairments [135, 398]. Audio captioning systems [398] generate descriptions of acoustic scenes for deaf and hard-of-hearing users, while audio description generation helps blind and low-vision users understand visual content through synchronized narration [390]. Audio Question Answering [338] enables natural-language interaction with sound scenes, and sign language synthesis combined with realistic avatar rendering supports communication for deaf communities [342]. These applications also underscore the importance of inclusive training data and evaluation across diverse user groups and acoustic conditions [82, 451].

sections.title: 8.4 Immersive Experience and Metaverse
sections.content: Immersive applications (e.g., XR/AR/VR) depend on sound and image staying coherent as the user moves, which ties rendering, spatial audio, and scene understanding more tightly than in flat media. Spatial Audio and Scene Rendering. Immersive experiences require audio that responds naturally to user movement and environmental context [64, 67]. Neural acoustic field methods [372] render spatial audio as function of listener position within reconstructed 3D scenes, enabling 6DoF audio with appropriate attenuation, occlusion, and room acoustics. AV-NeRF [326] and related approaches jointly model visual appearance and acoustic propagation, allowing users to navigate virtual environments while hearing spatially consistent sound. These techniques also support virtual concerts and live events, where audiences can experience performances from different virtual viewpoints [392]. However, deployment on head-mounted displays and spatial computing platforms requires low latency, often below 20ms, to avoid perceptual mismatch and motion sickness. Therefore, current research emphasizes efficient inference while preserving audio-visual consistency in dynamic and acoustically complex scenes [67]. 3D-Aware AV Interaction. Audio-Visual Language Maps [224] and related representations enable semantic understanding of 3D environments through combined audio and visual sensing [64, 67]. They support natural language queries about spatial scenes, such as locating ringing phone, and enable more context-aware interaction in virtual environments. Echo-based 3D reconstruction further complements visual sensing by using acoustic reflections to infer room geometry when line-of-sight is limited [372]. Metaverse applications additionally require seamless integration of user-generated content with synthesized environments [67]. Models that place sound sources at plausible 3D locations with physically consistent propagation can support collaborative creation in shared virtual spaces, although challenges remain in maintaining consistent rendering across devices and network conditions while meeting the low-latency demands of presence and embodiment [163].

sections.title: 8.5 Embodied AI and Robotics
sections.content: Physical platforms use vision for geometry and affordances while sound often marks goals, off-screen events, and contact; the two modalities rarely substitute for one another in deployment. Audio-Visual Navigation. Embodied agents operating in real-world environments benefit from multimodal perception that combines visual and acoustic information [64, 67]. The SoundSpaces platform [64] and follow-up work train agents to locate sound-emitting targets in complex 3D environments by using reverberation patterns, intensity gradients, and visual context [67]. Semantic audio-visual navigation extends this setting to object-level goals, such as locating ringing phone through joint reasoning over acoustic and visual evidence [224, 693]. For service robots and autonomous systems, acoustic sensing also complements vision in low-light conditions, under occlusion, and beyond the visual field [163]. Vision-Language-Action models [42, 268, 770] further integrate multimodal perception into end-to-end control policies that respond to verbal commands, environmental sounds, and manipulation feedback. Manipulation with Feedback. Acoustic contact sensing provides rich information about manipulation interactions that vision alone often cannot capture [163]. Sounds produced during contact can reveal surface material, slip events, and grasp stability, improving grasping, insertion, and assembly tasks when visual 50 feedback is limited [311, 520]. Human-robot interaction likewise benefits from audio-visual understanding of speech, gesture, and facial expression [603, 745], enabling more natural collaboration [41, 493]. In safety-critical applications, current research emphasizes real-time reliability, failure detection [336], and sim-to-real transfer under changing acoustic conditions [58, 163].

sections.title: 8.6 Ubiquitous Perception and Security
sections.content: Large-scale sensing for safety, industry, and the IoT still hinges on fusing what cameras and microphones can jointly establish about scenes, actors, and anomalies, often at the edge with tight compute budgets. Smart City and Forensics. Audio-visual event detection systems support public safety and urban monitoring [527]. Joint analysis of surveillance video and environmental audio improves detection of anomalous events such as accidents, altercations, and emergencies compared with single-modality systems [36, 527]. Audio-visual scene understanding can also localize sound sources within visual scenes, supporting incident reconstruction and situational awareness for first responders [36, 527]. In forensics, audio-visual analysis assists evidence authentication and investigation [108, 285]. Speaker recognition based on neural embeddings [128] enables voice identification, while deepfake detection methods examine the consistency between lip movements and speech to identify manipulated media [108]. As generation quality advances, detection methods must continue to improve to preserve trust in digital evidence [108]. Industry and IoT. Industrial applications employ audio-visual monitoring for predictive maintenance, quality control, and safety compliance [70, 168]. Acoustic anomaly detection identifies equipment faults from characteristic sound signatures [27, 83], while visual inspection detects defects in manufactured products. Combining both modalities improves reliability by correlating complementary indicators of system state [177, 417]. IoT deployments similarly integrate distributed audio-visual sensors for environmental monitoring, smart building management, and agriculture [478, 658]. Because edge deployment must satisfy tight power and compute budgets, efficient architectures are essential [121, 698]. Privacy-preserving pipelines that process data locally or transmit only semantic representations are also increasingly important for continuous sensing in public and private spaces [199, 609].

sections.title: 9 Open Challenges, Limitations, and Future Directions
sections.content: Audio-visual intelligence has progressed from deciding whether two modalities correspond, to generating synchronized videos and sounds, and further to systems that can hear, see, speak, edit, and act. The central difficulty, however, is not merely that audio and vision are different signal types. They are two partially observed views of dynamic world. Sounds are produced by events, propagate through space, interact with materials and occlusions, and are interpreted by users under conversational, social, and physical context. This makes AVI different from generic multimodal learning: the field must explain why audio and visual evidence co-occur, and how. Therefore, the next stage of AVI should not be framed as longer checklist of data, robustness, efficiency, and safety issues. These issues remain important, but they are too generic to define the field. more useful framing is research agenda for building audio-visual systems that are causal, contextual, controllable, verifiable, and interactive. The preceding sections reviewed perception, generation, and interaction as three pillars of AVI. Below we develop 6 major axes (cf. Table 24): causal event-source grounding, audio-visual world models, audio-visual context memory, causal audio-visual intervention, verifier and reward ecosystems, and interactive and responsible AVI. Figure 13 frames these axes as staged roadmap: the field is moving from correspondence, perception, and generation toward interactive systems, and then toward causal-contextual and verifiable agentic AVI. 51 Table 24 Six central research axes for future AVI. Each axis connects concrete limitation of current systems to structural capability required by audio-visual intelligence. Current dominant assumption Deeper AVI-specific objective Aspect Synchronization World modeling Audio and video are aligned if their embeddings, labels, or offsets match. Audio and vision are paired observations of the same clip. Context and memory Longer context windows or more tokens improve long-form AVI. Generation and editing Prompts specify desired audio-visual content. Evaluation FAD/FVD/CLIP/SyncNet-style metrics approximate quality and alignment. Interaction and deployment Omni models can extend static perception and generation directly. Model source-level, event-level, and causal alignment under delay, occlusion, off-screen sound, and multi-source mixtures. Treat audio and vision as complementary evidence for geometry, material, dynamics, affordance, and user/social state. Build selective, hierarchical, and provenance-aware audio-visual memory across streaming, episodic, and semantic levels. Support local, causal, and synchronized interventions over objects, sounds, identities, emotions, space, and time. Develop verifier and reward ecosystems for grounding, physical plausibility, audio indispensability, long-horizon coherence, and task utility. Balance real-time response, deliberative reasoning, user intent modeling, privacy, consent, and provenance in embodied and conversational AVI. Figure 13 Roadmap of AVI development stages.
