The first three stages consolidate correspondence, perception, and generation; the current frontier centers on interactive omni-modal and embodied systems; the next two stages emphasize causal-contextual AVI and verifiable agentic AVI. The associated research axes connect the discussion below to concrete future capabilities.

sections.title: 9.1 From Temporal Synchronization To Causal Event-Source Grounding
sections.content: Synchronization is often treated as local temporal matching problem: estimate whether the audio and video are aligned, or predict the offset between them. This formulation was effective for early lip-sync and synchronization systems, where mouth motion and speech are tightly coupled [108, 442], and it remains useful for measuring local correspondence in generated media. General AVI, however, requires richer notion of alignment. sound is not aligned with frame simply because the two embeddings are close. It is aligned with visual event if there is plausible source, temporal onset, propagation path, and causal relation between what is seen and what is heard. This distinction becomes critical beyond talking heads, especially in V2A, T2AV, AVQA, AV segmentation, and embodied perception. In practice, alignment is still often reduced to labels, offsets, or embedding proximity, which is insufficient when listeners must infer which physical event caused which sensory trace. Figure 14 Temporal synchronization checks offsets; causal grounding further infers sources, events, propagation, occlusion, and uncertainty. Figure 14 illustrates this shift: synchronization checks local temporal agreement, whereas event-source grounding asks which source produced which sound through which causal path. Denser Correspondence and Generation. Dense audiovisual correspondence and coordinated generation have improved: finer localization and retrieval emerge from dense features [204], and CAV-MAE Sync strengthens masked pretraining with explicit synchronization [23]. Event-aware soundtrack models such as Diff-Foley [374], FoleyCrafter [729], MMAudio [101], ThinkSound [348], HunyuanVideo-Foley [479], and Kling-Foley [554] move video-to-audio beyond generic ambience, while joint generators including JavisDiT [355], AV-DiT [556], UniVerse-1 [552], Harmony [220], Ovi [369], UniAVGen [711], and JavisDiT++ [356] co-synthesize both modalities rather than tacking sound onto video. Event-Source Graphs and Counterfactual Training. The next step is to treat scenes as event-source graphs: nodes for objects, latent sources, events, speakers, and environment; edges for production, temporal order, propagation paths, and uncertainty. Perception could then support questions about provenance and evidence; generation could plan coherent event sequences instead of weakly coupled streams; editing could specify what must follow when an object is removed or replaced. Current practice still confuses co-occurrence with causation; bark may appear with dog without knowing whether it is on-screen, delayed, off-screen, or muffled by geometry, and global metrics can stay high while source identity, timing, or propagation is wrong: SyncNet-style scores reward lip motion, not whether glass impact, rain density, or distant siren matches the scene. Training should therefore mix contrastive alignment with temporal boundaries, separation, interventions, and reconstruction on counterfactual-style supervision: visible-but-silent sources, audible off-screen events, shifted onsets, material conflicts, and mixtures where only one grounding is correct. The objective is not only to decide whether two modalities match, but to explain the event structure that makes them match.

sections.title: 9.2 From Paired Clips to Action-Conditioned Audio-Visual World Models
sections.content: Most current AVI datasets are built from paired audio-video clips. This form encourages correlation learning: guitar image co-occurs with guitar sound, speaking face co-occurs with speech, and car crash co-occurs with impact noise. Correlation is necessary, but it is not sufficient. Real audio-visual intelligence requires model of the world that can predict how visual and acoustic observations change when the agent moves, when an object is occluded, when room changes, or when an action is executed. This is especially important for embodied agents, XR, robotics, navigation, and manipulation, where the system must act rather than merely classify. Partial Progress in Simulation, Fields, Maps, and Touch. Pieces of such structure already exist: SoundSpaces [64] 53 and SoundSpaces 2.0 [67] support audio-visual navigation; Neural Acoustic Fields [372], AV-NeRF [326], AV-GS [38], and NeRAF [50] learn spatial acoustic or joint neural fields; Real Acoustic Fields [98] brings real impulse responses alongside visuals; Audio-Visual and Multimodal Spatial Language Maps [224, 225] fuse language with traversable spatial memory; ManiWAV [366], Sound of Simulation [566], and Audio-VLA [602] connect contact audio and generative acoustics to manipulation and transfer. Toward Hybrid Action-Conditioned Latent Dynamics. Yet rendering-centric fields, navigation benchmarks that treat sound only as goal cue, VLAs that bolt audio onto visionlanguage actions, and pixel-heavy video generators each stop short of shared predictive abstraction. The agenda is action-conditioned audiovisual world models that forecast compact latent state, including sources, materials, acoustics of the space, affordances, intent, and uncertainty, and support queries such as muffling behind wall, slip versus success sounds during grasping, louder sources after head turn, or co-evolving evidence if synthesized thunder enters clip. Hybrid stacks can combine learned latent dynamics with approximate acoustic physics and task-scoped scorers without rendering HD video every planning step. Evaluation should prioritize navigation gains, manipulation success, spatial reasoning, and counterfactual prediction rather than waveform or pixel reconstruction alone.

sections.title: 9.3 Audio-Visual Context Memory
sections.content: Semantic Span Is Not Raw Token Count. Longer multimodal contexts are often addressed by scaling windows or stuffing more tokens, which is the wrong abstraction when two-second sound matters more than hundreds of redundant frames. Omni conversational stacks (GPT-4o [239]; Qwen2.5-Omni [638]; Qwen3-Omni [639]; MingOmni [8]; InteractiveOmni [530]) illustrate unified interaction; unified representation lines (ImageBind [177], LanguageBind [761], OneLLM [205], AnyGPT [704], DenseAV [204]) add shared embeddings. Yet flattened token sequences still lose speaker identity, mix concurrent sources, or starve scarce audio evidence. Figure 15 uses OmniVideoBench duration splits [298] to show the same issue empirically: high short-video accuracy does not automatically translate to robust ultra-long-video accuracy. Layered AV Memory. Treat context as layered audio-visual memory: sensory buffers preserving recoverable fidelity where needed; event memory tracking onsets, tracks, masks, and positions; semantic summaries tethered to evidence; user/task state for goals and commitments, updated continuously without severing pointers to raw observations. Specialists (audio events, trackers, diarizers, temporal verifiers, intent models) should exchange structured messages rather than forcing one backbone to attend uniformly. Faithfulness is the bottleneck: brittle symbols, leaky summaries, and opaque embeddings each fail alone, so credible systems retain raw handles, dense retrieval, graphs, and summaries together, and benchmarks probe delayed recall, audio-critical QA, modality conflict, and belief revision. Figure 15 Duration robustness on OmniVideoBench. The x-axis shows short-video accuracy, the y-axis ultra-long-video accuracy, and the bubble area denotes overall accuracy; distance below the diagonal indicates long-context degradation.

sections.title: 9.4 Causal AV Intervention
sections.content: Controlled Edits Versus One-Shot Prompts. User intent in creation is rarely any plausible soundtrack: edits are local causal requests, such as removing only the guitar stem, rerouting spatialized sirens, or changing emotion without identity, that general prompts mis-specify. Video-to-audio models (Diff-Foley [374], MMAudio [101], ThinkSound [348], HunyuanVideo-Foley [479]) and audio-driven visuals (Wav2Lip [442], DiffTalk [483], EMO [525], VASA-1 [643], OmniAvatar [159], AudCast [188]) anchor one axis. Joint generators (JavisDiT [355], Harmony [220], Ovi [369], UniAVGen [711]) and localized editors (Object-AVEdit [156], AVI-Edit [744]) anchor another. Scene Graphs, Disentangled Latents, and Cross-Modal Feedback. Residual entanglement persists: prompts couple camera and identity; edits leak unrelated ambience or forget to update counterpart modalities. The path forward is causal editing via explicit or latent audio-visual scene graphs: objects, stems, identities, motions, causal links. Operations become graph interventions; generators must freeze what is untouched while propagating edits along dependencies. Practical control needs factorized latents separating stems, speech, ambience, identity, geometry, lighting, and camera so timing, ambience, and emotion edits decouple unless the task demands otherwise. Verifier-guided refinement only helps when feedback sees both modalities, otherwise visuals can sharpen while synchronization silently drifts. Open Problem: Editing Needs Causal Scope The hardest part of joint AV editing is deciding the scope of change. An edit should alter every consequence of the requested intervention, but preserve unrelated sources, identities, ambience, and narrative state. This requires models to reason over source-object bindings and downstream effects, not simply regenerate visually plausible clip.

sections.title: 9.5 Verifier and Reward Ecosystems
sections.content: Scalar Metrics Are Not Verifiers. Figure 16 summarizes the target stack: FID-style audio and video scores, semantic similarity, and lip-sync surrogates slice the problem [108, 177, 264, 538], yet none certify joint correctness: clip-level audio quality can coexist with wrong sources; prompt alignment may violate physics; lip sync can obscure mis-timed impacts. Understanding-centric suites such as AVUT [662], JointAVBench [62], OmniVideoBench [298], and AURA [157] broaden dependency and hallucination tests; generation-oriented benchmarks including JavisBench [355], VerseBench [552], PhyAVBench [629], and FoleyBench [131] push alignment and acoustics realism. Coverage is improving, but downstream utility ties remain uneven. Layered Verifiers as Evaluators and Training Rewards. Deploy staged verifier stack: signal fidelity; temporal synchronization; source grounding across visible, occluded, and offscreen cases; causal/physical coherence; inidenstruction or task payoff; plus safety, tity, and provenance. These models double as RL/DPO rerankers: recent preference-tuned audio-video pipelines [350, 351] hint at the appetite, though rewards must unpack faithFigure 16 Verifier and reward ecosystems decompose AVI correctness into signal, synchronization, grounding, causal/physical coherence, utility, safety, and provenance signals for evaluation and training. 55 fulness across modalities rather than collapsing to prettier frame. Treat narrow verifiers like narrow curricula: optimizing SyncNet alone can starve prosody or expression; over-relying on CLIP-like similarity excuses temporal hallucinations. Optimization should therefore be paired with adversarial/counterfactual probes and explicit failure taxonomies rather than leaderboard-only scores alone.

sections.title: 9.6 Interactive and Responsible AVI
sections.content: Interaction is AVIs stringent regime: latency, streaming evidence, interruptions, embodied safety, privacy. Static captioning or offline generation skips these constraints entirely. Omni Conversation and VLA-Style Action Interfaces. Realtime omni pipelines (GPT-4o [239], Qwen-Omni family [638, 639], VITA [154], Mini-Omni [631], Freeze-Omni [577], Ming-Omni [8], InteractiveOmni [530]) and architectures such as X-Streamer [630] emphasize streaming audiovisual world modeling; embodied stacks connect foundation perception to manipulation via OpenVLA [268], RT-2 [770], Octo [520], π0 [42], Audio-VLA [602], and Sound of Simulation [566]. Useful policies must schedule perception, reasoning, and actuation over time rather than concatenate encoders around an LLM. Fast Reactions, Deliberation, User Trust, and Safeguards. Couple fast reactive pathways, such as collisions and turn-taking, with verifier-backed deliberation for editing and planning and with uncertainty gates deciding when to ask, defer, or fetch memory. Sensitive user modeling (beliefs, affect, conversational roles) inevitably collides with privacy, consent, impersonation risks, continuous sensing, synthetic faces, and calibrated anthropomorphism. Mitigate by design with provenance, watermarking where appropriate, user-controlled memory partitioning, incidental-data minimization, and consent-aware synthesis of speech and avatars, evaluating on-device, federated, or redacted pipelines explicitly against accuracy and usability constraints. Overall, the frontier is not solely larger models or datasets but weaving together causal event-source grounding, audio-visual world models, audio-visual context memory, causal audio-visual intervention, verifier and reward ecosystems, and interactive and responsible AVI into systems that jointly explain multimodal evidence, forecast consequences under action, retain faithful memory, intervene with causal control, close the loop with multimodal verifiers, and deploy safely alongside humans.

sections.title: 10 Conclusion
sections.content: This survey provides comprehensive examination of audio-visual intelligence (AVI) in the era of foundation models, organizing the landscape around three interconnected pillars: representation-centric methods for signal embedding, generation-centric methods for cross-modal synthesis, and LLM-centric methods that utilize large language models as reasoning engines. Our review has traced the progression of audio-visual understanding from event recognition to complex spatial reasoning, while the generation survey highlighted the pivotal role of diffusion and autoregressive modeling in achieving synchronized, high-fidelity synthesis. central theme throughout is that cross-modal alignment remains the foundational challenge underlying these diverse tasks. Looking ahead, the field is moving toward unified architectures that handle perception and generation within single "omni" systems, yet this brings new hurdles in temporal synchronization at scale and training stability. Furthermore, as AVI extends into embodied robotics and immersive XR, the demands for efficiency, robustness, and safety become paramount. Ultimately, as these capabilities mature, addressing ethical risks such as deepfakes and privacy remains essential for the responsible development of AVI. We expect this survey to offer rigorous foundation for researchers navigating this rapidly evolving and consequential frontier of artificial intelligence.
